Adversarially review a generated LLD session. Be a skeptic — find REAL defects, do not rubber-stamp. Review-only: DO NOT modify files.

Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/b9b8118c-3795-43c8-b3ac-5bf4a6119a42_lld/  (source files + _response.md)
Run tests yourself: `cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/b9b8118c-3795-43c8-b3ac-5bf4a6119a42_lld && /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q`.

Problem: Design an Order Matching Engine. Required distinct angle: PRICE-TIME PRIORITY order book using TWO HEAPS (max-heap of bids, min-heap of asks) + FIFO within a price level + PARTIAL-FILL atomicity (a single submit may generate several trades and leave a remainder resting — all under one per-symbol lock so the book never tears) + PER-SYMBOL lock so different symbols match in parallel. Patterns: Strategy optional (order type), Information Expert (the book owns matching). Concurrency test: Barrier-sync many threads submitting crossing orders on ONE symbol; assert total filled quantity is conserved (sum of buys filled == sum of sells filled), no quantity created or destroyed, price-time priority respected, and the book is consistent (no crossed book left).

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence (file:line) on each:
1. CORRECTNESS of the core DS/algorithm: is the claimed structure actually implemented correctly (two-heap order book with price-time + partial fills / O(1) LFU buckets + min-freq pointer / trie + top-K / double-entry ledger conservation / state-machine legal transitions)? Find an input that breaks it.
2. CONCURRENCY CORRECTNESS: can the invariant be violated (money created/lost, crossed book, negative balance, cache over capacity, lost update, unsafe traffic state, debit-without-dispense)? Any deadlock, lost-wakeup, lock leak, or non-atomic check-then-act? Does rollback/release happen even when a step raises a NON-domain exception? Slow I/O outside the lock?
3. CONCURRENCY TEST STRENGTH: does it PROVE the invariant under real contention (Barrier + strict post-state: conservation AND uniqueness AND typed losers AND committed-state)? Or trivially passable (serialised, no hold, peak never reaches limit)? Injected Clock used where time matters?
4. §3.5 API section present with a real typed contract table (Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose)? Every method also in §4.1/§4.2/§6?
5. §4.1: does EVERY class subsection end with a COMPLETE fenced class block after its (c) table (method-less record still needs its fields block)?
6. Does the §6 ```python code match the actual disk files (no drift, no '...' bodies)?
7. EARN YOUR PATTERNS: are all claimed patterns genuinely realized in code?
8. MERMAID: exactly 2 blocks; NO semicolon ';' inside any sequenceDiagram message text; both would parse.
9. Python: 3.11+ idioms only, no bare except, one domain exception base, integer cents for money, no dead code.

Return verdict SHIP or FIX-FIRST and a findings list (severity/where/problem/fix). Only BLOCKER/MAJOR block; list MINORs too.

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Domain model for the order matching engine.
3	
4	The "nouns" of the exchange live here: enums, the typed exception family,
5	the immutable Trade record, the mutable Order entity, and the OrderBook for
6	ONE symbol (the Information Expert that owns price-time matching via two heaps).
7	"""
8	
9	import heapq
10	import itertools
11	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
12	from enum import Enum
13	
14	
15	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
16	# A trader either wants to BUY or SELL. This is the single most important field
17	# on an order — it decides which side of the book the order rests on and which
18	# side it can match against. We NEVER collapse it into a bool; a named side is
19	# what the interviewer wants to see survive end to end.
20	# 🎙️ "Side is BUY or SELL. A buy can only fill against asks, a sell against bids."
21	class Side(Enum):
22	    BUY = "BUY"
23	    SELL = "SELL"
24	
25	
26	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
27	# An order moves through a tiny lifecycle. We mark it explicitly so a caller can
28	# ask "did my order rest, partially fill, fully fill, or get cancelled?" without
29	# re-deriving it from quantities.
30	class OrderStatus(Enum):
31	    OPEN = "OPEN"                  # resting on the book, nothing filled yet
32	    PARTIALLY_FILLED = "PARTIAL"   # some quantity filled, remainder resting
33	    FILLED = "FILLED"              # fully filled, no longer on the book
34	    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"        # cancelled by the trader, remainder removed
35	
36	
37	# ── WHY ONE BASE EXCEPTION ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
38	# One base class for every failure this domain can raise. Callers can catch
39	# ExchangeError to handle "anything the engine rejected" and still pattern-match
40	# on the specific subclass. We NEVER raise a bare ValueError/RuntimeError from
41	# our own code — a typed error tells the caller exactly what went wrong.
42	class ExchangeError(Exception):
43	    """Base class for every error the matching engine raises."""
44	
45	
46	class UnknownSymbolError(ExchangeError):
47	    """A symbol was referenced that was never registered on the engine."""
48	
49	
50	class InvalidOrderError(ExchangeError):
51	    """An order failed validation — bad price, bad quantity, or bad side."""
52	
53	
54	class UnknownOrderError(ExchangeError):
55	    """A cancel referenced an order id the engine has never seen."""
56	
57	
58	class OrderNotCancellableError(ExchangeError):
59	    """A cancel hit an order that is already filled or already cancelled."""
60	
61	
62	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
63	# A Trade is the FACT that a buy and a sell met: who, how much, at what price.
64	# It never changes once printed, so it is frozen — once a trade is recorded the
65	# exchange has made a promise it cannot edit. Price is integer CENTS, never a
66	# float, because money rounding errors are unacceptable on an exchange.
67	# 🎙️ "A Trade is immutable — once two orders match, that print is permanent."
68	@dataclass(frozen=True)
69	class Trade:
70	    trade_id: str
71	    symbol: str
72	    buy_order_id: str
73	    sell_order_id: str
74	    price_cents: int               # the resting order's price sets the trade price
75	    quantity: int                  # shares exchanged in THIS fill (may be partial)
76	
77	
78	# ── WHY THIS ENTITY ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
79	# An Order is the live thing on the book. It owns its OWN remaining quantity and
80	# its OWN status — the book asks it to fill, it does the arithmetic on itself.
81	# It is mutable because remaining_qty shrinks as it fills; that is its whole job.
82	# 🎙️ "The Order tracks how much of it is left. When I fill it, IT decrements
83	#      its own remaining and flips its own status — no one reaches in and edits it."
84	@dataclass
85	class Order:
86	    order_id: str
87	    symbol: str
88	    side: Side
89	    price_cents: int               # limit price — integer cents
90	    quantity: int                  # original requested quantity (never changes)
91	    sequence: int                  # arrival rank — the TIME in price-time priority
92	    remaining_qty: int = field(init=False)
93	    status: OrderStatus = field(default=OrderStatus.OPEN, init=False)
94	
95	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
96	        # remaining starts equal to the full order; it only ever shrinks.
97	        self.remaining_qty = self.quantity
98	
99	    @property
100	    def filled_qty(self) -> int:
101	        # How much has been filled so far. Derived, never stored twice.
102	        return self.quantity - self.remaining_qty
103	
104	    @property
105	    def is_active(self) -> bool:
106	        # Active means it still has shares that could match or rest.
107	        return self.status in (OrderStatus.OPEN, OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED)
108	
109	    def fill(self, qty: int) -> None:
110	        # WHY: the order mutates ITSELF when filled. The book computes how many
111	        #      shares trade, then tells the order "you lost this many". The order
112	        #      guards its own invariant: you can never fill more than remains.
113	        if qty <= 0 or qty > self.remaining_qty:
114	            raise InvalidOrderError(
115	                f"cannot fill {qty} of order {self.order_id} "
116	                f"with {self.remaining_qty} remaining"
117	            )
118	        self.remaining_qty -= qty
119	
120	        if self.remaining_qty == 0:
121	            self.status = OrderStatus.FILLED
122	        else:
123	            self.status = OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED
124	
125	    def cancel(self) -> None:
126	        # WHY: cancelling just flips status. The book stops handing the order out
127	        #      once it is no longer active. We do NOT physically rip it out of the
128	        #      heap here — that is O(n); instead matching skips cancelled orders
129	        #      lazily when they reach the top. This keeps cancel O(1).
130	        self.status = OrderStatus.CANCELLED
131	
132	
133	# ── WHY THIS HELPER ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
134	# A heap of orders needs a strict ordering key. We never put the Order object
135	# itself on the heap (two orders are never "less than" each other); instead we
136	# push a tuple whose first elements ARE the price-time priority, and carry the
137	# order as payload. _HeapEntry makes that tuple comparison explicit and total.
138	@dataclass(order=True)
139	class _HeapEntry:
140	    sort_key: tuple[int, int]      # (price component, sequence) — compared first
141	    order: Order = field(compare=False)   # payload, never compared
142	
143	
144	# ── WHY THIS CLASS (THE INFORMATION EXPERT) ──────────────────────────────────
145	# The OrderBook owns ALL matching for ONE symbol. It is the Information Expert:
146	# it holds both sides, so it is the only thing that can decide whether two orders
147	# cross and in what order. The engine never reaches inside the book to match —
148	# it hands the book a new order and the book returns the trades.
149	#
150	# Two heaps give price-time priority in O(log n):
151	#   - bids: a MAX-heap on price (highest buyer first). Python heapq is a MIN-heap,
152	#     so we store -price to flip it.
153	#   - asks: a MIN-heap on price (lowest seller first) — heapq's natural order.
154	# Within one price, the heap's second key (sequence) breaks ties by EARLIEST
155	# arrival — that is the "time" in price-time priority, and it is FIFO.
156	# 🎙️ "The book is two heaps. Best bid is the highest price; best ask is the
157	#      lowest price. They cross when the best bid is at least the best ask."
158	class OrderBook:
159	    def __init__(self, symbol: str) -> None:
160	        self.symbol = symbol
161	        self._bids: list[_HeapEntry] = []     # max-heap by price (store -price)
162	        self._asks: list[_HeapEntry] = []     # min-heap by price
163	        # id → Order, so cancel can find an order and flip its status in O(1).
164	        self._orders_by_id: dict[str, Order] = {}
165	
166	    # ── best-of-book peeks (skip lazily-deleted dead orders) ──────────────────
167	
168	    def _peek_active(self, heap: list[_HeapEntry]) -> Order | None:
169	        # WHY: cancelled or fully-filled orders are left in the heap (lazy delete)
170	        #      and popped off the top only when we look. We discard dead tops here
171	        #      so the caller always sees a live best order or None.
172	        while heap and not heap[0].order.is_active:
173	            heapq.heappop(heap)
174	
175	        return heap[0].order if heap else None
176	
177	    def best_bid(self) -> Order | None:
178	        return self._peek_active(self._bids)
179	
180	    def best_ask(self) -> Order | None:
181	        return self._peek_active(self._asks)
182	
183	    # ── matching (the heart) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
184	
185	    def match(self, incoming: Order, trade_ids: "itertools.count[int]") -> list[Trade]:
186	        # WHY: a SINGLE incoming order may sweep MANY resting orders on the other
187	        #      side, generating several trades, then rest its remainder. All of
188	        #      that happens here in one call so the book never tears — the engine
189	        #      runs this whole method under one per-symbol lock.
190	        self._orders_by_id[incoming.order_id] = incoming
191	        trades: list[Trade] = []
192	
193	        opposite = self._asks if incoming.side is Side.BUY else self._bids
194	
195	        while incoming.remaining_qty > 0:
196	            resting = self._peek_active(opposite)
197	
198	            if resting is None or not self._crosses(incoming, resting):
199	                break
200	
201	            trades.append(self._execute(incoming, resting, trade_ids))
202	
203	        if incoming.remaining_qty > 0 and incoming.is_active:
204	            self._rest(incoming)
205	
206	        return trades
207	
208	    def _crosses(self, incoming: Order, resting: Order) -> bool:
209	        # WHY: a buy crosses a sell when the buyer will pay AT LEAST what the
210	        #      seller asks. A sell crosses a bid when the seller accepts AT MOST
211	        #      what the buyer offers. This is the "limit" in limit order.
212	        if incoming.side is Side.BUY:
213	            return incoming.price_cents >= resting.price_cents
214	
215	        return incoming.price_cents <= resting.price_cents
216	
217	    def _execute(
218	        self, incoming: Order, resting: Order, trade_ids: "itertools.count[int]"
219	    ) -> Trade:
220	        # WHY: the fill quantity is the smaller of the two remaining sizes — that
221	        #      is what makes a fill PARTIAL. The trade prints at the RESTING
222	        #      order's price (price-time priority: the order that waited sets the
223	        #      price, so the aggressor never gets a worse price than they asked).
224	        qty = min(incoming.remaining_qty, resting.remaining_qty)
225	        price = resting.price_cents
226	
227	        incoming.fill(qty)
228	        resting.fill(qty)
229	
230	        buy_id = incoming.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else resting.order_id
231	        sell_id = resting.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else incoming.order_id
232	
233	        return Trade(
234	            trade_id=f"trade-{next(trade_ids)}",
235	            symbol=self.symbol,
236	            buy_order_id=buy_id,
237	            sell_order_id=sell_id,
238	            price_cents=price,
239	            quantity=qty,
240	        )
241	
242	    def _rest(self, order: Order) -> None:
243	        # WHY: whatever did not match goes onto the book to wait. The sort key is
244	        #      (price, sequence). For bids we negate price so the MAX price sits
245	        #      at the heap top; for asks the natural MIN price sits on top. The
246	        #      sequence is the tie-breaker = earliest arrival wins at one price.
247	        if order.side is Side.BUY:
248	            key = (-order.price_cents, order.sequence)
249	            heapq.heappush(self._bids, _HeapEntry(key, order))
250	        else:
251	            key = (order.price_cents, order.sequence)
252	            heapq.heappush(self._asks, _HeapEntry(key, order))
253	
254	    # ── cancel ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
255	
256	    def cancel(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
257	        # WHY: look the order up by id, then ask IT to cancel itself. We do not
258	        #      remove it from the heap now (O(n)); matching skips it lazily.
259	        order = self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)
260	
261	        if order is None:
262	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found on {self.symbol}")
263	
264	        if not order.is_active:
265	            raise OrderNotCancellableError(
266	                f"order {order_id} is {order.status.value}, cannot cancel"
267	            )
268	
269	        order.cancel()
270	        return order
271	
272	    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order | None:
273	        return self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)
274	
275	    # ── invariant checks (used by tests and the demo) ─────────────────────────
276	
277	    def is_crossed(self) -> bool:
278	        # WHY: a healthy book is NEVER crossed — the best bid must be strictly
279	        #      below the best ask after matching settles. If best_bid >= best_ask
280	        #      we left money on the table (two orders that should have traded did
281	        #      not). The concurrency test asserts this is False.
282	        bid = self.best_bid()
283	        ask = self.best_ask()
284	
285	        if bid is None or ask is None:
286	            return False
287	
288	        return bid.price_cents >= ask.price_cents
289	
290	    def resting_quantity(self, side: Side) -> int:
291	        # WHY: total live shares resting on one side. Used to prove conservation:
292	        #      shares submitted == shares filled + shares still resting.
293	        heap = self._bids if side is Side.BUY else self._asks
294	        return sum(e.order.remaining_qty for e in heap if e.order.is_active)
295

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""The matching engine orchestrator.
3	
4	`MatchingEngine` is the clean, single-threaded core. It owns one OrderBook per
5	symbol and a global id->symbol index so a cancel can find any order. The public
6	methods (`submit_order`, `cancel_order`, `get_order`, `snapshot`) are the entire
7	external surface.
8	
9	Concurrency is a deliberate SECOND version: `ThreadSafeMatchingEngine` overrides
10	ONLY the `_lock` hook to take a PER-SYMBOL reentrant lock, so different symbols
11	match in parallel while everything touching ONE book serialises. The core file
12	below has zero lock code.
13	"""
14	
15	import itertools
16	import threading
17	from collections.abc import Iterator
18	from contextlib import contextmanager
19	
20	from models import (
21	    ExchangeError,
22	    InvalidOrderError,
23	    Order,
24	    OrderBook,
25	    Side,
26	    Trade,
27	    UnknownOrderError,
28	    UnknownSymbolError,
29	)
30	
31	
32	# ── WHY THIS CLASS (THE ORCHESTRATOR) ────────────────────────────────────────
33	# The engine is the public API. It does NOT match orders itself — it routes an
34	# incoming order to the right symbol's OrderBook (Information Expert) and returns
35	# the trades. It owns the symbol->book map, the id->symbol index for cancels, and
36	# the monotonic counters that stamp arrival sequence (the "time" in price-time).
37	# 🎙️ "The engine is a thin router. The book does the matching; the engine just
38	#      finds the right book, gives it the order, and hands back the trades."
39	class MatchingEngine:
40	    def __init__(self, symbols: list[str]) -> None:
41	        if not symbols:
42	            raise InvalidOrderError("engine needs at least one symbol")
43	
44	        # symbol -> its own independent book. Different symbols share NO state,
45	        # which is exactly why the thread-safe version can lock per symbol.
46	        self._books: dict[str, OrderBook] = {s: OrderBook(s) for s in symbols}
47	        # order id -> symbol, so cancel_order/get_order can route without the
48	        # caller telling us the symbol again.
49	        self._order_symbol: dict[str, str] = {}
50	        # Numbered ids make test assertions readable; UUIDs in production.
51	        # One counter stamps BOTH the order id and its arrival sequence so the
52	        # id "ord-7" and sequence 7 always agree — easy to read in tests.
53	        self._order_seq = itertools.count(1)
54	        self._trade_seq = itertools.count(1)
55	
56	    # ── concurrency hook (no-op in the clean core) ───────────────────────────
57	
58	    @contextmanager
59	    def _lock(self, symbol: str) -> Iterator[None]:
60	        # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version — there is zero lock code in
61	        #      this file. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this method with
62	        #      a real per-symbol lock. The diff between clean and safe is ~10 lines.
63	        yield
64	
65	    # ── public surface ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
66	
67	    def submit_order(
68	        self, symbol: str, side: Side, price_cents: int, quantity: int
69	    ) -> tuple[Order, list[Trade]]:
70	        # WHY: this is the pipeline — validate, build the order, match it against
71	        #      the book, rest the remainder. The whole match runs under one
72	        #      per-symbol lock so a single submit either fully resolves or not at
73	        #      all; the book never tears mid-sweep.
74	        self._validate(symbol, price_cents, quantity)
75	        book = self._books[symbol]
76	
77	        with self._lock(symbol):
78	            seq = next(self._order_seq)
79	            order = Order(
80	                order_id=f"ord-{seq}",
81	                symbol=symbol,
82	                side=side,
83	                price_cents=price_cents,
84	                quantity=quantity,
85	                sequence=seq,
86	            )
87	            self._order_symbol[order.order_id] = symbol
88	            trades = book.match(order, self._trade_seq)
89	
90	        return order, trades
91	
92	    def cancel_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
93	        # WHY: route by the id index to the owning book, then cancel under that
94	        #      book's lock so a cancel can never race a match on the same symbol.
95	        symbol = self._order_symbol.get(order_id)
96	
97	        if symbol is None:
98	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")
99	
100	        with self._lock(symbol):
101	            return self._books[symbol].cancel(order_id)
102	
103	    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
104	        # WHY: read the live order so a caller can inspect fill state. Routed via
105	        #      the same id index. A missing id is a typed error, not a None.
106	        symbol = self._order_symbol.get(order_id)
107	
108	        if symbol is None:
109	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")
110	
111	        order = self._books[symbol].get_order(order_id)
112	
113	        if order is None:
114	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")
115	
116	        return order
117	
118	    def snapshot(self, symbol: str) -> dict[str, int | bool | None]:
119	        # WHY: a read-only view of one book's top of book + resting depth. Used by
120	        #      the demo and tests to prove the book is consistent (not crossed).
121	        if symbol not in self._books:
122	            raise UnknownSymbolError(f"unknown symbol {symbol}")
123	
124	        book = self._books[symbol]
125	        bid = book.best_bid()
126	        ask = book.best_ask()
127	
128	        return {
129	            "best_bid": bid.price_cents if bid else None,
130	            "best_ask": ask.price_cents if ask else None,
131	            "bid_qty": book.resting_quantity(Side.BUY),
132	            "ask_qty": book.resting_quantity(Side.SELL),
133	            "crossed": book.is_crossed(),
134	        }
135	
136	    # ── validation helper ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
137	
138	    def _validate(self, symbol: str, price_cents: int, quantity: int) -> None:
139	        # WHY: reject bad input at the public boundary BEFORE we mutate any book.
140	        #      Every guard raises a specific typed error so the caller knows why.
141	        if symbol not in self._books:
142	            raise UnknownSymbolError(f"unknown symbol {symbol}")
143	
144	        if price_cents <= 0:
145	            raise InvalidOrderError(f"price must be positive, got {price_cents}")
146	
147	        if quantity <= 0:
148	            raise InvalidOrderError(f"quantity must be positive, got {quantity}")
149	
150	
151	# ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
152	# The thread-safe version. It overrides ONLY the `_lock` hook — every public
153	# method above is inherited UNCHANGED. We keep one RLock PER SYMBOL so two threads
154	# trading different symbols never block each other; only threads hitting the SAME
155	# symbol serialise. That is the whole concurrency design in one method.
156	# 🎙️ "Thread-safety is a 10-line subclass. One reentrant lock per symbol. Orders
157	#      on different symbols match in parallel; same-symbol orders take turns."
158	class ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(MatchingEngine):
159	    def __init__(self, symbols: list[str]) -> None:
160	        super().__init__(symbols)
161	        # One lock per symbol. We build them up front (symbols are fixed at
162	        # construction) so there is no map-mutation race; if symbols could be
163	        # added at runtime we would guard this dict with its own lock too.
164	        self._symbol_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {
165	            s: threading.RLock() for s in self._books
166	        }
167	
168	    @contextmanager
169	    def _lock(self, symbol: str) -> Iterator[None]:
170	        # WHY: RLock (reentrant) not Lock — a future guarded method might call
171	        #      another guarded method on the same symbol; an RLock lets the same
172	        #      thread re-enter instead of deadlocking on itself. Per symbol, not
173	        #      global, so AAPL and MSFT match at the same time.
174	        lock = self._symbol_locks.get(symbol)
175	
176	        if lock is None:
177	            # Defensive: an unknown symbol should already have been rejected by
178	            # _validate, but if it slips through we fail loud, not silently.
179	            raise ExchangeError(f"no lock for unknown symbol {symbol}")
180	
181	        with lock:
182	            yield
183

2	"""Narrated demo driver for the order matching engine.
3	
4	Run:  python main.py
5	Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK" so a green run is obvious.
6	Cases cover: a simple cross, a partial fill leaving a remainder resting,
7	price-time (FIFO) priority within a price level, cancel, validation errors,
8	and a tiny concurrency proof that the book stays consistent.
9	"""

11	import threading
12	
13	from exchange_service import MatchingEngine, ThreadSafeMatchingEngine
14	from models import (

16	    OrderNotCancellableError,
17	    OrderStatus,

19	    UnknownOrderError,
20	    UnknownSymbolError,
21	)

24	def case_1_simple_cross() -> None:
25	    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
26	    # A seller rests 100 @ $10.00, then a buyer crosses at $10.00.
27	    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)
28	    buy, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)
29	
30	    assert len(trades) == 1, trades
31	    assert trades[0].quantity == 100
32	    assert trades[0].price_cents == 1000          # prints at the resting price
33	    assert buy.status is OrderStatus.FILLED
34	    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
35	    assert snap["bid_qty"] == 0 and snap["ask_qty"] == 0
36	    assert snap["crossed"] is False
37	    print("Case 1 simple full cross at resting price -> OK")
38	
39	
40	def case_2_partial_fill() -> None:
41	    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
42	    # Seller has only 60; buyer wants 100 -> 60 trade, 40 of the buy rests.
43	    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=60)
44	    buy, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)
45	
46	    assert len(trades) == 1 and trades[0].quantity == 60
47	    assert buy.status is OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED
48	    assert buy.remaining_qty == 40
49	    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
50	    assert snap["best_bid"] == 1000 and snap["bid_qty"] == 40   # remainder rests
51	    assert snap["ask_qty"] == 0
52	    print("Case 2 partial fill leaves remainder resting -> OK")
53	
54	
55	def case_3_price_time_priority() -> None:
56	    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
57	    # Two sells at the SAME price; the EARLIER one must fill first (FIFO time).
58	    s_first, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=50)
59	    s_second, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=50)
60	    # A better-priced (lower) sell jumps the queue on PRICE.
61	    s_cheap, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=999, quantity=30)
62	
63	    _, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=60)
64	
65	    # Price first: the 999 sell (30) fills entirely, then the earliest 1000 sell.
66	    assert trades[0].sell_order_id == s_cheap.order_id and trades[0].quantity == 30
67	    assert trades[1].sell_order_id == s_first.order_id and trades[1].quantity == 30
68	    assert engine.get_order(s_first.order_id).remaining_qty == 20
69	    assert engine.get_order(s_second.order_id).remaining_qty == 50  # untouched
70	    print("Case 3 price-time priority: best price then earliest -> OK")
71	
72	
73	def case_4_cancel() -> None:
74	    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
75	    resting, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=950, quantity=40)
76	    engine.cancel_order(resting.order_id)
77	    assert engine.get_order(resting.order_id).status is OrderStatus.CANCELLED
78	
79	    # A crossing sell must NOT match the cancelled bid — it rests instead.
80	    sell, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=950, quantity=40)
81	    assert trades == []
82	    assert engine.snapshot("AAPL")["ask_qty"] == 40
83	    print("Case 4 cancel removes order from matching -> OK")

85	
86	def case_5_validation_and_errors() -> None:
87	    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
88	
89	    for bad_price in (0, -5):
90	        try:
91	            engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=bad_price, quantity=10)
92	            raise AssertionError("expected InvalidOrderError for bad price")
93	        except InvalidOrderError:
94	            pass
95	
96	    try:
97	        engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=0)
98	        raise AssertionError("expected InvalidOrderError for zero quantity")
99	    except InvalidOrderError:
100	        pass
101	
102	    try:
103	        engine.submit_order("TSLA", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
104	        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownSymbolError")
105	    except UnknownSymbolError:
106	        pass
107	
108	    try:
109	        engine.cancel_order("ord-999")
110	        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownOrderError")
111	    except UnknownOrderError:
112	        pass
113	
114	    # Cancelling an already-filled order is rejected.
115	    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
116	    buy, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
117	    try:
118	        engine.cancel_order(buy.order_id)
119	        raise AssertionError("expected OrderNotCancellableError")
120	    except OrderNotCancellableError:
121	        pass
122	
123	    print("Case 5 validation + typed errors -> OK")
124	
125	
126	def case_6_concurrency_conservation() -> None:
127	    # Many threads slam crossing orders on ONE symbol. We seed a big resting buy,
128	    # then race sells against it. No shares may be created or destroyed, and the
129	    # book must not be left crossed.
130	    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
131	    n_threads = 40
132	    qty_each = 10
133	    # One large resting bid that can absorb everything: 40 * 10 = 400 shares.
134	    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=n_threads * qty_each)
135	
136	    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
137	    all_trades: list = []
138	    lock = threading.Lock()
139	
140	    def seller(_i: int) -> None:
141	        barrier.wait()
142	        _order, trades = engine.submit_order(
143	            "AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=qty_each
144	        )
145	        with lock:
146	            all_trades.extend(trades)
147	
148	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=seller, args=(i,)) for i in range(n_threads)]
149	    for t in threads:
150	        t.start()
151	    for t in threads:
152	        t.join()
153	
154	    filled = sum(t.quantity for t in all_trades)
155	    assert filled == n_threads * qty_each, f"shares not conserved: {filled}"
156	    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
157	    assert snap["crossed"] is False
158	    assert snap["bid_qty"] == 0 and snap["ask_qty"] == 0   # all consumed exactly
159	    print(f"Case 6 concurrency: {filled} shares filled, book consistent -> OK")
160	
161	
162	def case_7_per_symbol_parallelism() -> None:
163	    # Different symbols share no state, so they match independently.
164	    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL", "MSFT"])
165	    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
166	    engine.submit_order("MSFT", Side.SELL, price_cents=2000, quantity=5)
167	    _, t_aapl = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
168	    _, t_msft = engine.submit_order("MSFT", Side.BUY, price_cents=2000, quantity=5)
169	
170	    assert t_aapl[0].symbol == "AAPL" and t_aapl[0].price_cents == 1000
171	    assert t_msft[0].symbol == "MSFT" and t_msft[0].price_cents == 2000
172	    print("Case 7 per-symbol books are independent -> OK")
173	
174	
175	def main() -> None:
176	    case_1_simple_cross()
177	    case_2_partial_fill()
178	    case_3_price_time_priority()
179	    case_4_cancel()
180	    case_5_validation_and_errors()
181	    case_6_concurrency_conservation()
182	    case_7_per_symbol_parallelism()
183	    print("\nAll cases passed.")
184	
185	
186	if __name__ == "__main__":

199	def test_concurrent_crossing_conserves_quantity() -> None:
200	    # N seller threads each submit a crossing SELL of qty_each on ONE symbol, all
201	    # released at one instant by the Barrier so they truly race the same book.
202	    # A single big resting BUY can absorb everything.
203	    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
204	    n_threads = 50

211	    guard = threading.Lock()

215	        _order, trades = engine.submit_order(

217	        )

1	# Design an Order Matching Engine
2	
3	## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan
4	
5	| Time | What you do | Mode |
6	|------|-------------|------|
7	| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate the problem (limit BUY/SELL, per-symbol book, price-time priority, partial fills, cancel, thread-safe per symbol). Ask the 5-6 clarifiers. Do not write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
8	| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — state the 4 public calls (`submit_order`, `cancel_order`, `get_order`, `snapshot`) with args/returns/raises. "Before classes, here's the contract." | 🗣️ SAY |
9	| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — 4 boxes that matter: `MatchingEngine`, `OrderBook`, `Order`, `Trade`. Mention `Side`/`OrderStatus` enums verbally. | ✍️ DRAW |
10	| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — Information Expert (the book owns matching), two-heaps data structure. Then flag: "There's a check-then-act race inside `match` — a single submit must sweep the book atomically. I'll fix it with a per-symbol lock at the end." | 🗣️ SAY |
11	| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — the two-heap price-time book, partial-fill atomicity, lazy cancel. | 🗣️ SAY |
12	| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (§6.0): enums → `Order` (self-filling entity) → `OrderBook` (two heaps + `match`) → `MatchingEngine` skeleton → `submit_order` → `cancel_order`. | ✍️ WRITE |
13	| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 3** — full cross, then price-time priority. Mode B: run `main.py`. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
14	| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-symbol RLock via the no-op `_lock` hook; the whole `match` sweep is the critical section so the book never tears. Barrier test proves conservation. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
15	| 55–60 min | **Follow-up questions** — distributed (sharded by symbol), 10M orders, persistence/replay. | ❓ IF ASKED |
16	
17	**Two interview modes:**
18	- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output; demo cases verbally.
19	- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN `main.py` in §7. This write-up is Mode B — every snippet below actually ran.
20	
21	**The one thing that flags you "strong hire":** right after the diagram, say "a single `submit` may generate several trades and leave a remainder resting — that whole sweep has to be atomic, or two concurrent submits tear the book. I'll guard it with one per-symbol lock." Saying that proactively signals you've built matching/concurrent systems.
22	
23	---
24	
25	## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions
26	
27	### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)
28	
29	> "This is the engine at the core of a stock exchange. Traders send in limit orders — 'buy 100 shares of AAPL at no more than $10.00' or 'sell 50 at no less than $9.95'. The engine keeps an order book per symbol and whenever a new order can cross an existing one — a buyer willing to pay at least what a seller asks — it matches them and prints a trade. Matching follows price-time priority: the best price goes first, and within the same price the order that arrived earliest goes first. A match can be partial — a 100-share buy might eat a 60-share sell and then rest the leftover 40 on the book waiting for more."
30	
31	It's a real-world system most candidates haven't built, so I'll state my mental model and let the interviewer correct me: orders are limit orders (they carry a price ceiling/floor), the book is in memory, and we match continuously on every new arrival.
32	
33	### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)
34	
35	**You:** "I'll assume limit orders only for v1 — every order carries a price. Market orders (fill at any price) are a small extension I can mention later. Sound right?"
36	**Interviewer:** "Yes, limit only is fine."
37	*✓ scoped: every order has a price; market orders deferred to §9 extensibility.*
38	
39	**You:** "Matching is continuous — I match on every new arrival against the resting book, not in batched auctions. And a trade prints at the RESTING order's price, since that order set the price first. OK?"
40	**Interviewer:** "Agreed — resting price, continuous matching."
41	*✓ locked: aggressor never gets a worse price than its limit; passive order's price is the print price.*
42	
43	**You:** "Price-time priority: best price first, then earliest arrival (FIFO) within a price level. A single incoming order can sweep multiple resting orders and partially fill the last one. That's the core. Good?"
44	**Interviewer:** "That's the heart of it, yes."
45	*✓ this is the whole matching contract — drives the two-heap design.*
46	
47	**You:** "Cancel just removes a resting order's remaining quantity from matching. If it's already fully filled, cancel is rejected — there's nothing left to cancel. Reasonable?"
48	**Interviewer:** "Yep. Partially-filled orders can still be cancelled for the remainder."
49	*✓ cancel acts on the live remainder; filled/cancelled orders reject with a typed error.*
50	
51	**You:** "In-memory, single process. Thread-safe PER SYMBOL — different symbols are completely independent books so they should match in parallel; only orders on the same symbol serialize. I'll keep the core single-threaded-correct and add a thread-safe variant at the end. OK?"
52	**Interviewer:** "Exactly what I want to see — per-symbol concurrency, not one global lock."
53	*✓ the headline concurrency decision: per-symbol lock, parallel across symbols.*
54	
55	**You:** "I'll treat order modification (change price/qty) as cancel-then-resubmit, and skip persistence, fees, and market data feeds for this round. Agreed?"
56	**Interviewer:** "Agreed."
57	*✓ scope cut: no modify/persistence/fees — clean modeling room.*
58	
59	### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)
60	
61	**Functional Requirements**
62	
63	1. **Submit a limit order** (symbol, side BUY/SELL, price, quantity)
64	   - Engine matches it against the opposite side by price-time priority, generating zero or more trades
65	   - Any unmatched remainder rests on the book; returns the order plus the list of trades
66	   - Rejects unknown symbol, non-positive price, non-positive quantity with typed errors
67	2. **Match crossing orders by price-time priority**
68	   - Best price first (highest bid / lowest ask); within a price, earliest arrival first (FIFO)
69	   - A trade prints at the resting (passive) order's price
70	3. **Support partial fills atomically**
71	   - One submit may fill several resting orders and leave its own remainder resting — all in one indivisible step so the book is never observed half-matched
72	4. **Cancel a resting order** by id
73	   - Removes the live remainder from future matching; returns the cancelled order
74	   - Rejects unknown id (`UnknownOrderError`) and already-filled/cancelled id (`OrderNotCancellableError`)
75	5. **Inspect state** — look up an order by id; snapshot a symbol's top-of-book and resting depth (used to prove the book is never crossed)
76	6. **Thread-safe per symbol** — concurrent submits/cancels on one symbol are serialized; different symbols match in parallel
77	
78	**Out of scope**
79	- Market orders / stop orders (limit-only for v1 — easy Strategy extension, §9)
80	- Order modification (modeled as cancel + resubmit)
81	- Persistence, crash recovery, trade settlement, fees (downstream systems)
82	- Market-data dissemination / matching across a network (single in-memory process)
83	
84	> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK — before I model anything, the one thing I want to lock down is that matching is continuous and price-time, with the trade printing at the resting order's price. With that, let me walk through the actors and flows."
85	
86	---
87	
88	## 2. Actors & Core Flows
89	
90	**Actors**
91	- **Trader** — submits BUY/SELL limit orders and cancels resting orders. The only human actor.
92	- **MatchingEngine** — system actor that owns the per-symbol books and routes every order to the right one.
93	- **OrderBook** — system actor (one per symbol) that actually matches and rests orders.
94	
95	**Core flows**
96	1. Trader: submit order → engine routes to symbol's book → book matches against opposite side → trades returned, remainder rests
97	2. Trader: submit crossing order → book sweeps multiple resting orders → several trades, last one possibly partial → done
98	3. Trader: cancel(order_id) → engine routes to book → order flips to CANCELLED → skipped by future matching
99	4. Trader: snapshot(symbol) → engine reads top-of-book and resting depth (best bid, best ask, not crossed)
100	
101	Each flow is one line; the detailed walk is §6 and the sequence diagram is §8.
102	
103	---
104	
105	## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
106	
107	### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject)
108	
109	> **Order** — A trader's intent: side, price, quantity, and how much is still unfilled. It has its OWN lifecycle (OPEN → PARTIALLY_FILLED → FILLED, or → CANCELLED) and owns the arithmetic of shrinking its remaining quantity as it fills. *Accepted — it's the central mutable entity.*
110	>
111	> **OrderBook** — The per-symbol structure that holds resting bids and asks and decides who matches whom. It owns both heaps and the matching algorithm. This is the Information Expert — it's the only thing that sees both sides, so it's the only thing that can match. *Accepted.*
112	>
113	> **Trade** — The fact that a buy met a sell: which two orders, what price, how many shares. It never changes after it's printed. *Accepted — an immutable (frozen) value object.*
114	>
115	> **MatchingEngine** — The orchestrator. Owns the map of symbol → book and the id → symbol index for routing cancels. It doesn't match itself; it routes. *Accepted as the orchestrator.*
116	>
117	> **Symbol** — A ticker like "AAPL". It has no behavior — it's just a key into the book map. *Rejected as entity — modeled as a `str` key.*
118	>
119	> **Side / OrderStatus** — Finite sets of values (BUY/SELL; OPEN/PARTIAL/FILLED/CANCELLED) with no behavior of their own. *Rejected as entities — modeled as enums.*
120	>
121	> **Price / Quantity** — Just integer fields on an Order. Price is integer CENTS (never a float — money rounding on an exchange is unacceptable). *Rejected — fields, not entities.*
122	>
123	> **Trader** — An actor who calls the engine; the engine doesn't store traders. *Rejected — lives outside the model.*
124	>
125	> **PriceLevel** — Tempting: a bucket of all orders at one price, FIFO inside. It would be a real entity in a production engine (a `dict[price → deque]`). For this design the two-heap approach folds the price-level ordering into the heap's sort key `(price, sequence)`, so I don't need a separate class. *Rejected for v1 — its job is absorbed by the heap key; I'd introduce it at scale (§9).*
126	
127	### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)
128	
129	| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
130	|---|---|---|---|
131	| `MatchingEngine` | Route each order to the right symbol's book; own routing indexes | `_books: dict[str, OrderBook]`, `_order_symbol: dict[str, str]` (id→symbol, for cancel routing), `_order_seq/_trade_seq: count` | Every order id maps to exactly one symbol; ids are unique and monotonic |
132	| `OrderBook` | Match and rest orders for ONE symbol by price-time priority | `_bids: list[_HeapEntry]` (max-heap), `_asks: list[_HeapEntry]` (min-heap), `_orders_by_id: dict[str, Order]` | After matching settles the book is never crossed (best_bid < best_ask) |
133	| `Order` | Track its own remaining quantity and status as it fills | `order_id: str` (**id**), `side: Side`, `price_cents: int`, `quantity: int`, `sequence: int`, `remaining_qty: int`, `status: OrderStatus` | Never fills more than `remaining_qty`; remaining only shrinks |
134	| `Trade` (frozen) | Record one fill permanently | `trade_id: str` (**id**), `buy_order_id/sell_order_id: str` (refs to the two Orders), `price_cents: int`, `quantity: int` | Immutable once printed — a trade is a permanent promise |
135	
136	**Enums**
137	- `Side` — `BUY`, `SELL`
138	- `OrderStatus` — `OPEN`, `PARTIALLY_FILLED`, `FILLED`, `CANCELLED`
139	
140	### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro
141	
142	> "So the model is four objects. `MatchingEngine` is the router — it owns the map of symbol to book and an id-to-symbol index so a cancel can find any order without the caller repeating the symbol. `OrderBook` is the interesting one — it's the Information Expert that owns matching for one symbol, because it's the only thing holding both sides of the book, so it's the only thing that can decide who crosses whom. `Order` is the live entity — it remembers how much of itself is left and flips its own status as it fills; nobody reaches in and edits it. `Trade` is a frozen record — once a buy and a sell meet, that print is permanent. The nouns I deliberately left OUT are Symbol, which is just a string key, Price and Quantity, which are integer fields, and PriceLevel — its FIFO-within-a-price job is folded into the heap's sort key, so I only introduce a separate level class at large scale."
143	
144	---
145	
146	## 3.5 API / System Interface
147	
148	The orchestrator's public surface — the four calls external code makes. Everything else (matching, resting, heap maintenance) is internal to the book.
149	
150	| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
151	|---|---|---|---|---|
152	| `submit_order` | `(symbol: str, side: Side, price_cents: int, quantity: int) -> tuple[Order, list[Trade]]` | the new order plus every trade it generated | `UnknownSymbolError`, `InvalidOrderError` | A trader sends a limit order; engine matches it and rests the remainder |
153	| `cancel_order` | `(order_id: str) -> Order` | the cancelled order | `UnknownOrderError`, `OrderNotCancellableError` | A trader pulls a resting order's remainder out of matching |
154	| `get_order` | `(order_id: str) -> Order` | the live order (fill state) | `UnknownOrderError` | Inspect an order's status and remaining quantity |
155	| `snapshot` | `(symbol: str) -> dict[str, int \| bool \| None]` | best bid/ask, resting depths, crossed flag | `UnknownSymbolError` | Read-only top-of-book view; proves the book isn't crossed |
156	
157	> 🎙️ "The whole system is four calls: one that takes orders in and does the matching (`submit_order`), one that pulls them out (`cancel_order`), and two read-only inspectors. If you handed me this interface cold, you'd know exactly what an exchange engine does without seeing a single class — orders go in, trades come out, and you can cancel or peek."
158	
159	---
160	
161	## 4. Class Design
162	
163	### 4.1 Per-class derivation
164	
165	#### `MatchingEngine` (the orchestrator)
166	
167	**(a) One-line role** — the public API: external code calls it to submit, cancel, and inspect. It routes each order to the right symbol's book and never matches itself.
168	
169	**(b) State derivation**
170	
171	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
172	|---|---|
173	| "maintains a per-symbol order book" | `_books: dict[str, OrderBook]` |
174	| "support cancel" (route by id without re-passing symbol) | `_order_symbol: dict[str, str]` (order id → symbol) |
175	| "earliest arrival first" + readable ids | `_order_seq: count` (arrival rank = the "time"), `_trade_seq: count` |
176	
177	```
178	class MatchingEngine:
179	    - _books: dict[str, OrderBook]
180	    - _order_symbol: dict[str, str]
181	    - _order_seq: itertools.count
182	    - _trade_seq: itertools.count
183	```
184	
185	**State placement trade-off** — the arrival `sequence` (the "time" in price-time) could live on the Order or be stamped by the engine. I stamp it in the engine because the engine is the single serialization point that sees the true arrival order across the whole symbol; that's *relational* state (rank among all orders), not *physical* state of one order, so the orchestrator owns it.
186	
187	**(c) Operations derivation**
188	
189	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
190	|---|---|
191	| "traders submit limit BUY/SELL orders" | `submit_order(symbol, side, price, qty) -> (Order, list[Trade])` |
192	| "support cancel" | `cancel_order(order_id) -> Order` |
193	| inspect an order's fill state | `get_order(order_id) -> Order` |
194	| prove the book is consistent | `snapshot(symbol) -> dict` |
195	| guard the per-symbol concurrency seam | `_lock(symbol)` (no-op hook) |
196	
197	```
198	class MatchingEngine:
199	    - _books: dict[str, OrderBook]
200	    - _order_symbol: dict[str, str]
201	    - _order_seq: itertools.count
202	    - _trade_seq: itertools.count
203	
204	    + MatchingEngine(symbols: list[str])
205	    + submit_order(symbol, side, price_cents, quantity) -> tuple[Order, list[Trade]]
206	    + cancel_order(order_id: str) -> Order
207	    + get_order(order_id: str) -> Order
208	    + snapshot(symbol: str) -> dict[str, int | bool | None]
209	    # _lock(symbol) -> contextmanager      (no-op; overridden in ThreadSafe subclass)
210	    # _validate(symbol, price_cents, quantity) -> None
211	```
212	
213	**(d) Design choices**
214	- **Why an id→symbol index instead of asking each book?** A cancel only knows the order id. Without the index, cancel would scan every book O(symbols). The index makes routing O(1).
215	- **Why return `(Order, list[Trade])` from submit?** The caller needs both: their order's resulting state (filled? resting?) and the trades it caused (for their fill report). Returning only trades would hide whether the remainder rested.
216	- **Why is `_lock` a method that does nothing?** So the clean core has zero lock code. The thread-safe subclass overrides only this one hook — the diff is ~10 lines.
217	
218	#### `OrderBook` (the Information Expert)
219	
220	**(a) One-line role** — owns matching for ONE symbol. It holds both sides and is the only thing that can decide who crosses whom.
221	
222	**(b) State derivation**
223	
224	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
225	|---|---|
226	| "best price first" for buyers | `_bids`: max-heap of resting buys (store `-price`) |
227	| "best price first" for sellers | `_asks`: min-heap of resting sells |
228	| "support cancel" by id | `_orders_by_id: dict[str, Order]` |
229	
230	```
231	class OrderBook:
232	    - symbol: str
233	    - _bids: list[_HeapEntry]   # max-heap by price, then earliest sequence
234	    - _asks: list[_HeapEntry]   # min-heap by price, then earliest sequence
235	    - _orders_by_id: dict[str, Order]
236	```
237	
238	**State placement trade-off** — the resting-order collections are *physical* state of the book (which orders are physically sitting on this symbol's book), so they live on `OrderBook`, not the engine. The engine only knows "symbol → book"; it never reaches into the heaps.
239	
240	**(c) Operations derivation**
241	
242	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
243	|---|---|
244	| "matches crossing orders by price-time priority" + partial fills | `match(incoming, trade_ids) -> list[Trade]` |
245	| "support cancel" | `cancel(order_id) -> Order` |
246	| top-of-book peeks for matching and snapshots | `best_bid() -> Order \| None`, `best_ask() -> Order \| None` |
247	| prove never crossed / conserved | `is_crossed() -> bool`, `resting_quantity(side) -> int` |
248	| look up for the engine | `get_order(order_id) -> Order \| None` |
249	
250	```
251	class OrderBook:
252	    - symbol: str
253	    - _bids: list[_HeapEntry]
254	    - _asks: list[_HeapEntry]
255	    - _orders_by_id: dict[str, Order]
256	
257	    + OrderBook(symbol: str)
258	    + match(incoming: Order, trade_ids: count) -> list[Trade]
259	    + cancel(order_id: str) -> Order
260	    + best_bid() -> Order | None
261	    + best_ask() -> Order | None
262	    + get_order(order_id: str) -> Order | None
263	    + is_crossed() -> bool
264	    + resting_quantity(side: Side) -> int
265	    # _peek_active(heap) -> Order | None    (skips lazily-deleted dead tops)
266	    # _crosses(incoming, resting) -> bool
267	    # _execute(incoming, resting, trade_ids) -> Trade
268	    # _rest(order) -> None
269	```
270	
271	**(d) Design choices**
272	- **Why two heaps, not one sorted list?** A sorted list is O(n) to insert. A heap is O(log n) for push and pop, and we only ever need the BEST order, which is exactly the heap top.
273	- **Why lazy cancel (leave dead orders in the heap)?** Removing an arbitrary order from a heap is O(n). Instead `cancel` flips the order's status and `_peek_active` discards dead tops when they surface. Cancel stays O(1); matching pays a tiny amortized cost.
274	- **Why does the trade print at the RESTING price?** The resting order set its price first and waited; price-time priority says the aggressor takes that price (and never worse than its own limit).
275

448	## 5. Design Patterns & Principles
449	
450	### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)
451	
452	> "I followed Information Expert throughout. `OrderBook` owns matching because it's the only object holding both the bid and ask heaps — the decision 'do these two cross?' needs both sides, and the book is the only thing that sees both. `Order` enforces its own fill rule (never fill more than remains) because that invariant is intrinsic to the order. `MatchingEngine` owns only routing and id allocation, because it's the single point that sees all symbols and the true global arrival order. Each class owns the state and behavior closest to its own data — that's why there's no 'manager' class reaching into someone else's fields."
453	
454	### 5.2 Applied patterns
455	
456	**Template Method — the concurrency hook**
457	- **Why it fits HERE:** the clean matching core must stay lock-free and readable, but I still need a single, well-defined place to add per-symbol locking. `MatchingEngine._lock(symbol)` is a no-op context manager in the core; `ThreadSafeMatchingEngine` overrides only that method with a per-symbol `RLock`. The core never changes.
458	- **Interface + concrete:** `MatchingEngine._lock` (no-op) ← `ThreadSafeMatchingEngine._lock` (per-symbol RLock).
459	- **🎙️ Script:** "Concurrency is one overridden method. The core has zero lock code; the thread-safe version is a ten-line subclass."
460	- **💬 Layman gloss:** "It's like leaving an empty slot labelled 'lock goes here'. The fast version leaves it empty; the safe version drops a real lock in. Nothing else moves."
461	
462	That's the only GoF pattern that's genuinely load-bearing. The rest of the design is Information Expert plus a frozen value object — and that's a stronger answer than name-dropping a Strategy I don't use.
463	
464	**Strategy — extension point (NOT wired in v1):** order-type matching rules (market/stop) would become a `MatchingRule` strategy. I'm not pretending it's in the code; it's the §9 extension seam at `_crosses`.
465	
466	### 5.3 SOLID principles
467	
468	- **Single Responsibility:** `OrderBook` owns matching, `Order` owns its own fill state, `MatchingEngine` owns routing, `Trade` just records. Four reasons to change, four classes.
469	- **Open/Closed:** a new order type is a new crossing rule at the `_crosses` seam (§9), not edits to the engine. Thread-safety is a subclass, not an edit to the core.
470	- **Dependency Inversion (the concurrency seam):** `submit_order` depends on the abstract `_lock` hook, not on `threading.RLock` directly — the lock implementation is injected by subclassing.
471	
472	### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS
473	
474	Every claim survives Cmd-F: `Template Method` is the real `_lock` override in `ThreadSafeMatchingEngine`; `Information Expert` is `OrderBook.match` and `Order.fill`; `Immutable Value Object` is `@dataclass(frozen=True)` on `Trade`. I do **not** claim Strategy or Factory — they aren't in v1. I explicitly label the order-type Strategy as an extension point, not an applied pattern.

478	## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach
479	
480	### A. The Key Insight
481	
482	> **Insight:** Represent the book as TWO heaps — a max-heap of bids and a min-heap of asks, keyed by `(price, arrival-sequence)` — and make a single `submit` sweep the opposite heap in a loop, partially filling the last order, all under one per-symbol lock.
483	
484	> **Why it works:** Price-time priority is exactly a total order on `(price, time)`: best price first, ties broken by earliest arrival. A heap gives you the single best element in O(log n) and lets you pop it in O(log n) — and "best" is all matching ever needs. Negating the bid price turns Python's min-heap into a max-heap. The arrival sequence as the second key gives FIFO within a price level for free, no separate queue needed.
485	
486	> **What breaks without it:** The naive version keeps each side as a list and re-sorts (or linear-scans for the best) on every order — O(n log n) per submit, and a subtle bug: if you sort only by price you lose FIFO and orders at the same price fill in arbitrary order, which is unfair and non-deterministic. Worse, if you don't treat the whole sweep as one atomic step, a partial fill that spans three resting orders can be observed half-done by a concurrent reader, and you end up with a crossed book — best bid ≥ best ask — which means two orders that should have traded are both sitting there. The two-heap + atomic-sweep design makes both bugs impossible.
487	
488	### B. Per-Operation Algorithm
489	
490	#### `submit_order` / `OrderBook.match` (the pipeline)
491	
492	**(1) Pseudocode**
493	```
494	1. validate: symbol known, price > 0, quantity > 0   (else typed error)
495	2. stamp arrival sequence, build the Order (remaining = quantity)
496	3. record id -> symbol for routing
497	4. >>> enter per-symbol lock (critical section) <<<
498	5. loop:
499	6.    resting = best order on the OPPOSITE side (skip cancelled/filled tops)
500	7.    if no resting order OR it does not cross -> break
501	8.    qty = min(incoming.remaining, resting.remaining)
502	9.    incoming.fill(qty); resting.fill(qty)     # each guards its own invariant
503	10.   print Trade at the RESTING order's price
504	11. if incoming has remainder AND is still active -> push it onto its own heap (rest)
505	12. >>> exit lock <<<
506	13. return (incoming, trades)
507	```
508	
509	**(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script — the pipeline:** "This is the pipeline — validate, then sweep, then rest. Step 6 is non-obvious: I peek the best of the OPPOSITE heap, and `_peek_active` lazily throws away any cancelled or filled order sitting on top — that's how cancel stays O(1). Step 7 is where price-time priority fires: `_crosses` checks the limit, and because the heap top is already the best price and earliest arrival, I never have to search. Step 8 — `min` of the two remainings — is what makes a fill PARTIAL. Step 10 prints at the RESTING price, not the incoming price; that's the rule I locked down in §1.2. Step 11 is the 'leave the remainder resting' requirement. The interviewer will probe step 9 — 'what if two submits hit at once?' — and that's exactly why steps 4–12 are one critical section."
510	
511	**(3) DRY RUN — `submit_order("AAPL", BUY, price=1002, qty=60)` against asks `[S1@1000×20, S2@1001×20, S3@1002×20]`:**
512	
513	| Step | Action | State change |
514	|---|---|---|
515	| 2 | build BUY ord-4, remaining=60 | — |
516	| 6 | best ask = S1@1000 (lowest price) | — |
517	| 7 | 1002 ≥ 1000 → crosses | — |
518	| 8-10 | qty=min(60,20)=20; fill both; print trade @1000 | ord-4 remaining 60→40; S1 FILLED; trade-1=20@1000 |
519	| 6 | best ask now S2@1001 (S1 popped as dead) | — |
520	| 8-10 | qty=20; print @1001 | ord-4 remaining 40→20; S2 FILLED; trade-2=20@1001 |
521	| 6 | best ask now S3@1002 | — |
522	| 8-10 | qty=20; print @1002 | ord-4 remaining 20→0, FILLED; S3 FILLED; trade-3=20@1002 |
523	| 7 | remaining=0 → loop ends | — |
524	| 13 | return | (ord-4 FILLED, [trade-1, trade-2, trade-3]) |
525	
526	> *Edge probe: a second BUY submits while step 8 is mid-flight?* Without the lock both could read S1 as having 20 left and both fill it — S1 over-fills (its `fill` guard would raise, but on the *other* thread, corrupting that submit). §9 wraps steps 4–12 in a per-symbol RLock so the whole sweep is atomic.
527	
528	#### `cancel_order` (the redemption path)
529	
530	**(1) Pseudocode**
531	```
532	1. look up symbol from order id index   (else UnknownOrderError)
533	2. >>> enter per-symbol lock <<<
534	3. find order in book by id             (else UnknownOrderError)
535	4. if not active (filled/cancelled) -> OrderNotCancellableError
536	5. order.cancel()    # flips status; do NOT rip out of heap (O(n))
537	6. >>> exit lock <<<
538	7. return order
539	```
540	
541	**(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough — cancel is a lookup, a guard, and a flag.** "Two guards and a flag. Step 4 is the one the interviewer probes: cancelling an already-filled order is rejected with a typed error, not silently ignored — there's nothing left to cancel. Step 5 is the clever-but-cheap part: I don't remove the order from the heap, that's O(n); I just flip its status, and matching skips it lazily when it surfaces at the top (look back at step 6 of `match` — `_peek_active` discards it). Cancel stays O(1)."
542	
543	#### `snapshot` / `get_order` (lookups)
544	
545	**🎙️ These are O(1)/O(1)-amortized index hits.** "`get_order` is a dict hit on the id index — that's why I keep `_orders_by_id` on the book. `snapshot` peeks both heap tops (amortized O(1) after discarding dead tops) and sums resting depth; it exists to PROVE the book isn't crossed, which is the assertion my concurrency test leans on."
546	
547	**Coverage:** every method in §4 — `submit_order`/`match`, `cancel_order`/`cancel`, `get_order`, `snapshot`, `best_bid`/`best_ask`, `is_crossed`, `resting_quantity`, `fill`, `_crosses`, `_execute`, `_rest`, `_peek_active` — is represented above.
548	
549	### C. Data Structure Choices
550	
551	| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
552	|---|---|---|---|
553	| `list[_HeapEntry]` as max-heap (bids, `-price`) and min-heap (asks) | resting orders by price-time | heap gives the single BEST order in O(log n); a sorted list is O(n) insert and a plain list is O(n) to find the best. We only ever need the top. | push/pop O(log n), peek O(1) |
554	| `(price, sequence)` tuple as heap key | price-time priority + FIFO | folds the price-level FIFO into the heap key — no separate `dict[price → deque]` needed at this scale. Sequence as 2nd key = earliest-arrival tie-break for free. | comparison O(1) |
555	| `dict[order_id → Order]` (per book) | cancel / get_order by id | id is the natural key; the alternative was scanning the heaps on every cancel — O(n). Dict makes lookup O(1). | O(1) |
556	| `dict[order_id → symbol]` (engine) | route cancel without re-passing symbol | the alternative was scanning every book for the id — O(symbols × n). Index makes routing O(1). | O(1) |
557	| lazy delete (flag, don't remove) | cancel | removing an arbitrary element from a heap is O(n); flagging is O(1) and matching discards dead tops when they surface. | cancel O(1), amortized into match |
558	
559	I chose the SIMPLER two-heap-with-tuple-key over a `dict[price → deque]` price-level book. **Threshold to flip:** at very high depth where many orders cluster at one price and lazy-deleted entries pile up, a `dict[price → deque[Order]]` (a "price level ladder") avoids the heap re-heapify cost and makes cancel a true O(1) removal — I'd switch around tens of thousands of resting orders per symbol.
560	
561	### D. Tricky Logic
562	
563	> **🧠 Claim:** The trade must print at the RESTING order's price, not the incoming order's.
564	> **🔬 Proof:** A buy comes in at $10.05 and the best resting ask is $10.00. If I print at $10.05 the buyer overpays and the price-time-priority promise ("you never get worse than your limit, and the passive order's price stands") is broken.
565	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `_execute` sets `price = resting.price_cents`, never `incoming.price_cents`.
566	
567	> **🧠 Claim:** A single submit's multi-fill-plus-rest must be ATOMIC.
568	> **🔬 Proof:** A 60-share buy sweeps S1(20)+S2(20)+S3(20). If a concurrent reader peeks after S1 fills but before S2, it sees a half-swept book — possibly still crossed. Worse, a concurrent *writer* could double-fill S2.
569	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `submit_order` runs the entire `book.match(...)` call inside `with self._lock(symbol)`. In the core that's a no-op; in `ThreadSafeMatchingEngine` it's a real per-symbol RLock.
570	
571	> **🧠 Claim:** Cancel must be O(1) and matching must skip cancelled orders.
572	> **🔬 Proof:** If cancel removed from the heap it'd be O(n); if matching didn't skip cancelled orders a cancelled bid could still match an incoming sell — a phantom trade.
573	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Order.cancel()` only flips status; `_peek_active` pops any `not is_active` order off the heap top before returning the best, so cancelled orders never match.
574	
575	> **🧠 Claim:** An order can never be over-filled.
576	> **🔬 Proof:** A rounding or logic bug could try to fill 30 shares of an order with 20 remaining, creating 10 phantom shares — quantity created from nothing.
577	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Order.fill(qty)` raises `InvalidOrderError` if `qty > remaining_qty`. The book always passes `min(incoming.remaining, resting.remaining)`, so it can't happen — but the entity guards it anyway.
578	
579	> **🧠 Claim:** The book must never be left crossed after matching settles.
580	> **🔬 Proof:** If matching stopped early (a bug in the cross check), best_bid could be ≥ best_ask — two orders that should have traded both resting.
581	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `match` loops until the best opposite order no longer crosses; `is_crossed()` asserts the invariant and the concurrency test checks it after the race.
582	
583	### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example
584	
585	> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
586	> ```
587	> def submit_order(self, symbol, side, price, qty):
588	>     book = self._books[symbol]
589	>     for resting in sorted(book.opposite(side), key=lambda o: o.price):  # re-sort every time
590	>         if crosses(side, price, resting.price):
591	>             traded = min(qty, resting.qty)
592	>             resting.qty -= traded        # direct field write, no guard
593	>             qty -= traded
594	>     if qty > 0:
595	>         book.opposite_of(side).append(Order(...))   # rest leftover, no ordering
596	> ```
597	> **Why it's wrong:** Three failures. (1) Sorting by price ALONE drops FIFO — same-price orders fill in arbitrary order, which is unfair and non-deterministic. (2) Direct `resting.qty -= traded` bypasses the entity's over-fill guard. (3) Re-sorting every submit is O(n log n); a heap is O(log n) per operation. The correct version peeks the heap top (best price + earliest arrival baked into the key), calls `order.fill()` so the entity guards itself, and rests the remainder back on the heap.

751	
752	```python copy
753	@dataclass
754	class Order:
755	    order_id: str
756	    symbol: str
757	    side: Side
758	    price_cents: int
759	    quantity: int
760	    sequence: int
761	    remaining_qty: int = field(init=False)
762	    status: OrderStatus = field(default=OrderStatus.OPEN, init=False)
763	
764	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
765	        self.remaining_qty = self.quantity   # starts full, only shrinks
766	
767	    @property
768	    def filled_qty(self) -> int:
769	        return self.quantity - self.remaining_qty
770	
771	    @property
772	    def is_active(self) -> bool:
773	        return self.status in (OrderStatus.OPEN, OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED)
774	
775	    def fill(self, qty: int) -> None:
776	        # WHY: the order guards its OWN invariant — never fill more than remains.
777	        if qty <= 0 or qty > self.remaining_qty:
778	            raise InvalidOrderError(
779	                f"cannot fill {qty} of order {self.order_id} "
780	                f"with {self.remaining_qty} remaining"
781	            )
782	        self.remaining_qty -= qty
783	        self.status = OrderStatus.FILLED if self.remaining_qty == 0 else OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED
784	
785	    def cancel(self) -> None:
786	        # WHY: just flip status; matching skips it lazily. Cancel stays O(1).
787	        self.status = OrderStatus.CANCELLED
788	```

790	> 🎙️ "The order owns its own arithmetic. The book computes how many shares trade and calls `fill`; the order decrements itself and flips its own status. The over-fill guard makes it impossible to create phantom shares."

792	### `OrderBook` — two heaps + match (the heart)
793	
794	The private comparison wrapper so heapq has a total order, then the book.
795	
796	```python copy
797	@dataclass(order=True)
798	class _HeapEntry:
799	    sort_key: tuple[int, int]              # (price component, sequence)
800	    order: Order = field(compare=False)    # payload, never compared
801	```
802	
803	> 🎙️ "I never put two Orders on a heap directly — one order isn't 'less than' another. I push a tuple `(price, sequence)` and carry the order as payload that's excluded from comparison."
804	
805	```python copy
806	class OrderBook:
807	    def __init__(self, symbol: str) -> None:
808	        self.symbol = symbol
809	        self._bids: list[_HeapEntry] = []      # max-heap by price (store -price)
810	        self._asks: list[_HeapEntry] = []      # min-heap by price
811	        self._orders_by_id: dict[str, Order] = {}
812	
813	    def _peek_active(self, heap: list[_HeapEntry]) -> Order | None:
814	        # WHY: cancelled/filled orders are left in the heap (lazy delete) and
815	        #      discarded only when they reach the top. Caller always sees a live best.
816	        while heap and not heap[0].order.is_active:
817	            heapq.heappop(heap)
818	
819	        return heap[0].order if heap else None
820	
821	    def best_bid(self) -> Order | None:
822	        return self._peek_active(self._bids)
823	
824	    def best_ask(self) -> Order | None:
825	        return self._peek_active(self._asks)
826	```
827	
828	**`match` — role:** the one method that owns price-time priority and partial fills for this symbol.
829	
830	Core logic:
831	1. Register the incoming order by id
832	2. Repeatedly take the best opposite order
833	3. If none or it doesn't cross, stop
834	4. Trade `min(remainings)` at the resting price
835	5. Rest any remainder
836	
837	Edge cases: opposite side empty; best opposite doesn't cross; incoming fully filled mid-sweep; incoming partially filled then rests.
838	
839	```python copy
840	    def match(self, incoming: Order, trade_ids: "itertools.count[int]") -> list[Trade]:
841	        # WHY: one incoming order may sweep MANY resting orders and then rest its
842	        #      remainder — all here, so the engine can wrap the whole thing in one
843	        #      per-symbol lock and the book is never observed half-swept.
844	        self._orders_by_id[incoming.order_id] = incoming
845	        trades: list[Trade] = []
846	        opposite = self._asks if incoming.side is Side.BUY else self._bids
847	
848	        while incoming.remaining_qty > 0:
849	            resting = self._peek_active(opposite)
850	
851	            if resting is None or not self._crosses(incoming, resting):
852	                break
853	
854	            trades.append(self._execute(incoming, resting, trade_ids))
855	
856	        if incoming.remaining_qty > 0 and incoming.is_active:
857	            self._rest(incoming)
858	
859	        return trades
860	```
861	
862	> 🎙️ **Say (pipeline):** "Sweep the opposite side while the incoming still has shares AND the best opposite order crosses. Each pass prints one trade. Whatever's left rests."
863	> ⚠️ "What if two submits hit the same symbol at once?" → "That's the race I flagged. The engine wraps this whole `match` in a per-symbol lock, so a submit is all-or-nothing — no one sees the book half-swept."
864	
865	```python copy
866	    def _crosses(self, incoming: Order, resting: Order) -> bool:
867	        # WHY: a buy crosses when it'll pay AT LEAST the ask; a sell crosses when
868	        #      it accepts AT MOST the bid. This is the 'limit' in limit order.
869	        if incoming.side is Side.BUY:
870	            return incoming.price_cents >= resting.price_cents
871	        return incoming.price_cents <= resting.price_cents
872	```
873	
874	#### Approach — `_execute` (the most interesting helper: what price, what quantity?)
875	
876	**Approach 1 — fill at the incoming order's price (rejected):**
877	```
878	qty = min(incoming.remaining, resting.remaining)
879	trade_price = incoming.price_cents
880	```
881	> **Challenges:** A buy at $10.05 hitting a resting ask at $10.00 would print at $10.05 — the buyer overpays and the passive order's price is ignored. This breaks the price-time-priority promise; the passive order set the price first.
882	
883	**Approach 2 — split a partial fill into two trades to "even it out" (rejected for this scale):**
884	```
885	half = qty // 2
886	emit Trade(half) at resting; emit Trade(qty-half) at incoming
887	```
888	> **Challenges:** Invents trades that never happened — one fill is one trade. Doubles trade volume and confuses downstream settlement. Pure over-engineering.
889	
890	**Chosen — fill `min(remainings)` at the RESTING price, one trade per fill:**
891	```python copy
892	    def _execute(self, incoming, resting, trade_ids):
893	        qty = min(incoming.remaining_qty, resting.remaining_qty)   # partial-fill size
894	        price = resting.price_cents                                # passive price wins
895	        incoming.fill(qty)
896	        resting.fill(qty)
897	        buy_id = incoming.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else resting.order_id
898	        sell_id = resting.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else incoming.order_id
899	        return Trade(f"trade-{next(trade_ids)}", self.symbol, buy_id, sell_id, price, qty)
900	```
901	> **Why it wins:** `min` of the two remainings is exactly the largest fill both can honor — that IS the partial fill. The resting price is the price-time rule. One fill, one immutable trade — clean and correct.
902	
903	```python copy
904	    def _rest(self, order: Order) -> None:
905	        # WHY: negate price for bids so the MAX price sits on the heap top;
906	        #      asks use natural MIN. Sequence is the FIFO tie-break at one price.
907	        if order.side is Side.BUY:
908	            heapq.heappush(self._bids, _HeapEntry((-order.price_cents, order.sequence), order))
909	        else:
910	            heapq.heappush(self._asks, _HeapEntry((order.price_cents, order.sequence), order))
911	
912	    def cancel(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
913	        order = self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)
914	        if order is None:
915	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found on {self.symbol}")
916	        if not order.is_active:
917	            raise OrderNotCancellableError(f"order {order_id} is {order.status.value}, cannot cancel")
918	        order.cancel()
919	        return order
920	
921	    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order | None:
922	        return self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)
923	
924	    def is_crossed(self) -> bool:
925	        # WHY: a healthy book is NEVER crossed after matching settles.
926	        bid, ask = self.best_bid(), self.best_ask()
927	        if bid is None or ask is None:
928	            return False
929	        return bid.price_cents >= ask.price_cents
930	
931	    def resting_quantity(self, side: Side) -> int:
932	        heap = self._bids if side is Side.BUY else self._asks
933	        return sum(e.order.remaining_qty for e in heap if e.order.is_active)
934	```
